bytes.topic: Data Exploration — profiling datasets, understanding distributions, missing values
bytes.title: Polars describe() and null_count() — Profile Any Dataset in Seconds
bytes.body:
Polars gives you fast dataset profiling without reaching for external libraries. Start with df.describe() — it returns count, null_count, mean, std, min, max, and median for every column in a single pass, leveraging Polars' multi-threaded engine. Unlike pandas, this runs in parallel across columns automatically.

For missing value analysis, df.null_count() returns a single-row DataFrame with null counts per column — far cleaner than chaining .is_null().sum(). Combine it with df.schema to quickly flag columns where nulls exceed a threshold.

To understand distributions, use df.select(pl.col('price').quantile(q)) across multiple quantiles, or value_counts() for categorical columns. The n_unique() expression helps identify low-cardinality columns suitable for encoding.

For a full profiling pass, chain these in a single select() context. Polars' lazy evaluation means you can build the entire profile as a LazyFrame and call .collect() once, avoiding redundant scans over the same data.
bytes.code_snippet:
import polars as pl

df = pl.read_csv("dataset.csv")

# Full statistical summary
print(df.describe())

# Null analysis per column
nulls = df.null_count()
print(nulls)

# Distribution profiling in one pass
profile = df.select(
    pl.all().n_unique().name.suffix("_nunique"),
    pl.all().null_count().name.suffix("_nulls"),
    pl.selectors.numeric().mean().name.suffix("_mean"),
    pl.selectors.numeric().std().name.suffix("_std"),
)

bytes.topic: Pandas Fundamentals — DataFrames, Series, indexing, groupby, merge/join
bytes.title: Polars GroupBy + Join — The Pandas Patterns You Already Know, Rewritten
bytes.body:
Polars shares Pandas' core abstractions — DataFrame, Series, groupby, join — but the API is expression-based and lazy-first. There's no index. Rows are accessed by position or filtered by expression, never by label. `df.select()` and `df.filter()` replace `.loc[]` and `.iloc[]`.

GroupBy returns an intermediate that takes expressions, not lambdas: `df.group_by('team').agg(pl.col('score').mean())` is the Polars equivalent of `df.groupby('team')['score'].mean()`. The key difference: you can pack multiple aggregations into a single `.agg()` call without renaming gymnastics.

Joins use `df.join(other, on='key', how='inner')` — same semantics as Pandas `merge()`, but Polars defaults to suffix `_right` and doesn't create a MultiIndex. For non-equi joins, use `.join_where()`.

Series is a single typed column. Unlike Pandas, there's no implicit type coercion — a `pl.Int64` Series rejects strings at insertion, not at query time. This strictness catches bugs earlier and keeps lazy query plans optimizable.
bytes.code_snippet:
import polars as pl

df = pl.DataFrame({
    "team": ["a", "a", "b", "b"],
    "score": [10, 20, 30, 40],
    "bonus": [1, 2, 3, 4],
})

# groupby with multiple aggs in one call
result = df.group_by("team").agg(
    pl.col("score").mean().alias("avg_score"),
    pl.col("bonus").sum().alias("total_bonus"),
)

# join two frames on a shared key
lookup = pl.DataFrame({"team": ["a", "b"], "region": ["US", "EU"]})
joined = result.join(lookup, on="team", how="inner")
print(joined)

bytes.topic: NumPy — array operations, broadcasting, vectorization vs loops, memory layout
bytes.title: Polars + NumPy — When to Drop Into Array Operations and When to Stay in Expressions
bytes.body:
Polars expressions handle most vectorized work, but sometimes you need NumPy's broadcasting or specialized array ops. The bridge is `.to_numpy()` on a Series and `pl.from_numpy()` back. Key insight: Polars stores columns as Arrow arrays (column-major, contiguous memory), so `.to_numpy(zero_copy_only=True)` avoids allocation when dtypes align — Float64, Int64, and other numeric types get a zero-copy view. This breaks if the column has nulls, since Arrow uses a validity bitmap that NumPy can't represent.

For broadcasting: if you need element-wise ops across multiple columns that Polars expressions can't express cleanly (e.g., custom matrix math), pull columns into a 2D NumPy array, compute, and push back. But avoid this for simple arithmetic — `pl.col('a') * pl.col('b')` uses SIMD-optimized Arrow compute kernels that beat NumPy's generic ufuncs on columnar data.

Rule of thumb: use NumPy for scipy/linalg interop and bulk array reshaping. Stay in Polars expressions for filtering, aggregation, and column arithmetic — the query optimizer can't see through NumPy calls.
bytes.code_snippet:
import polars as pl
import numpy as np

df = pl.DataFrame({"feature": [1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 4.0], "weight": [0.1, 0.5, 0.3, 0.1]})

# Zero-copy to NumPy for scipy interop
arr = df.get_column("feature").to_numpy(zero_copy_only=True)
normalized = (arr - arr.mean()) / arr.std()  # NumPy broadcasting

# Push back into Polars
df = df.with_columns(pl.Series("feature_norm", normalized))

# Prefer expressions for simple ops (optimizer-friendly)
df = df.with_columns(
    (pl.col("feature") * pl.col("weight")).alias("weighted")
)

bytes.topic: Polars — lazy evaluation, expressions API, performance vs Pandas, when to switch
bytes.title: Polars Lazy API — Why collect() Is the Only Line That Costs You
bytes.body:
Polars' lazy evaluation rewrites your entire query plan before touching a single row. When you call .lazy(), nothing executes — Polars builds a logical plan. Calling .collect() triggers the optimizer: predicate pushdown filters rows before joins, projection pushdown drops unused columns, and common subexpression elimination deduplicates work. This is why a chained lazy pipeline often outperforms the equivalent eager Pandas code by 5–20x on datasets over 1M rows.

The expressions API is the key differentiator. Instead of row-wise apply(), Polars expressions like pl.col('price').over('category').mean() compile to vectorized, multi-threaded operations on Arrow-backed columns. No GIL, no copies.

When to switch from Pandas: if you're calling .apply() with a Python lambda on more than 100K rows, if you're chaining multiple groupby-aggregations, or if memory pressure forces you to process in chunks. Polars handles all three natively — streaming mode even processes larger-than-RAM datasets lazily.
bytes.code_snippet:
import polars as pl

df = pl.scan_parquet("events.parquet")  # returns LazyFrame

result = (
    df
    .filter(pl.col("status") == "completed")
    .with_columns(
        pl.col("revenue").over("region").mean().alias("avg_region_rev")
    )
    .group_by("region")
    .agg(
        pl.col("revenue").sum().alias("total"),
        pl.col("user_id").n_unique().alias("unique_users"),
    )
    .sort("total", descending=True)
    .collect()  # optimizer runs here
)

bytes.topic: Data Cleaning — handling nulls, outlier detection, type coercion, deduplication
bytes.title: Polars Data Cleaning — Nulls, Outliers, Types, and Dedup in One Pipeline
bytes.body: Polars expressions let you chain an entire cleaning pipeline without intermediate DataFrames. For nulls, `fill_null` with a strategy like `forward` or `mean` keeps things lazy-friendly, but `drop_nulls` on specific columns is often cleaner than blanket removal. Outlier detection fits naturally into `filter` — compute z-scores with `(col - col.mean()) / col.std()` and threshold inline. Type coercion via `cast` is strict by default; use `strict=False` to coerce failures to null rather than panicking, then handle those nulls downstream. Deduplication with `unique` accepts a `keep` parameter (`first`, `last`, `none`) and a `subset` of columns — critical when your key isn't the full row. The real power: all of this composes in a single `lazy()` pipeline. The query optimizer reorders and fuses operations, so writing readable step-by-step transforms costs you nothing at execution time. Always prefer `lazy` mode for cleaning — eager frames skip the optimizer entirely.
bytes.code_snippet:
import polars as pl

df = pl.scan_csv("raw_data.csv")

cleaned = (
    df
    .with_columns(
        pl.col("price").cast(pl.Float64, strict=False),
        pl.col("category").fill_null("unknown"),
    )
    .filter(
        ((pl.col("price") - pl.col("price").mean()) / pl.col("price").std()).abs() < 3
    )
    .unique(subset=["product_id", "timestamp"], keep="last")
    .drop_nulls(subset=["price"])
    .collect()
)

bytes.topic: Feature Engineering — encoding categoricals, scaling, binning, interaction features
bytes.title: Polars Feature Engineering — Categoricals, Scaling, and Binning Without Leaving Lazy Mode
bytes.body: Polars expressions make feature engineering composable and fast — no need to drop into pandas or sklearn. For one-hot encoding, cast to `Categorical` then use `to_dummies()` on the DataFrame. For target/label encoding, use a `group_by` aggregation joined back. Numerical scaling is a single expression: `(col - col.mean()) / col.std()` for z-score, or `(col - col.min()) / (col.max() - col.min())` for min-max. Binning uses `cut()` or `.over()` with `qcut()` for quantile bins. Interaction features are trivial — multiply or concatenate columns inline with `with_columns`. The key advantage: all of these work in lazy mode, so Polars optimizes the entire pipeline before execution. Chain them in a single `with_columns` block to avoid redundant passes. Use `Enum` dtype over `Categorical` when your categories are fixed and known ahead of time — it's stricter and catches invalid values at cast time rather than silently adding them.
bytes.code_snippet:
import polars as pl

df = pl.LazyFrame({"city": ["NYC","SF","NYC","LA"], "price": [100,250,150,90], "sqft": [500,800,600,400]})

result = df.with_columns(
    # min-max scaling
    ((pl.col("price") - pl.col("price").min()) / (pl.col("price").max() - pl.col("price").min())).alias("price_scaled"),
    # quantile binning
    pl.col("sqft").qcut(3, labels=["small","mid","large"]).alias("size_bin"),
    # interaction feature
    (pl.col("price") / pl.col("sqft")).alias("price_per_sqft"),
    # label encoding via rank
    pl.col("city").cast(pl.Categorical).to_physical().alias("city_encoded"),
).collect()

print(result)

bytes.topic: Exploratory Data Analysis — correlation analysis, pivot tables, aggregation patterns
bytes.title: Polars Pivot Tables and Correlation — EDA Patterns That Actually Scale
bytes.body:
Polars makes exploratory aggregation brutally fast by pushing operations into its lazy query engine. For correlation analysis, skip manual loops — use `df.select(pl.corr())` to compute Pearson coefficients between numeric columns in a single expression. Need a correlation matrix? Combine `pl.corr()` across column pairs inside a `select` context.

Pivot tables in Polars use `df.pivot()`, which reshapes long-form data by specifying `on`, `index`, and `values` with an aggregate function. Unlike pandas, Polars requires an explicit aggregate — no silent defaults.

For grouped aggregation patterns, chain `.group_by().agg()` with expression lists. Polars lets you pack multiple aggregations into one pass: `pl.col('price').mean()`, `pl.col('price').std()`, `pl.col('id').n_unique()` — all in a single `.agg()` call. The lazy engine fuses these into one scan.

Key EDA trick: call `.describe()` first for summary stats, then use `.group_by().agg()` with `.sort()` to surface outlier groups before plotting.
bytes.code_snippet:
import polars as pl

df = pl.DataFrame({
    "category": ["A", "B", "A", "B", "A", "B"],
    "region": ["US", "US", "EU", "EU", "US", "EU"],
    "revenue": [100, 200, 150, 300, 120, 280],
    "cost": [40, 90, 60, 130, 50, 120],
})

# Correlation between numeric columns
corr = df.select(pl.corr("revenue", "cost")).item()
print(f"Revenue-Cost correlation: {corr:.3f}")

# Pivot table: avg revenue by category × region
pivot = df.pivot(on="region", index="category", values="revenue", aggregate_function="mean")
print(pivot)

# Multi-stat aggregation in one pass
stats = df.group_by("category").agg(
    pl.col("revenue").mean().alias("avg_revenue"),
    pl.col("cost").sum().alias("total_cost"),
    pl.len().alias("count"),
).sort("avg_revenue", descending=True)
print(stats)

bytes.topic: Matplotlib — figure/axes API, customization, subplots, publication-quality charts
bytes.title: Polars + Matplotlib — Publication-Quality Charts Without Pandas Detours
bytes.body:
Polars DataFrames don't plug directly into Matplotlib, but you don't need `.to_pandas()` either. Extract columns as native Python lists or NumPy arrays via `.to_numpy()` and feed them straight into the figure/axes API.

Use `fig, axes = plt.subplots()` over `plt.plot()` — the OO interface gives you explicit control over each axis, which matters when you're plotting multiple Polars aggregations side-by-side. For subplots, chain Polars group-by results into separate axes: compute your aggregation with `df.group_by().agg()`, then iterate axes.

Key customization wins: `ax.set_xlabel()` with LaTeX math mode for scientific labels, `ax.tick_params()` for readable tick styling, and `fig.savefig('chart.pdf', bbox_inches='tight')` for camera-ready output. Set `plt.rcParams['font.family'] = 'serif'` globally for publication consistency.

Polars' `.to_numpy(zero_copy_only=True)` avoids memory copies on numeric columns — critical when plotting millions of points. This keeps your visualization pipeline as zero-copy as your data pipeline.
bytes.code_snippet:
import polars as pl
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

df = pl.read_csv("benchmarks.csv")
stats = df.group_by("framework").agg(
    pl.col("latency_ms").mean().alias("avg"),
    pl.col("latency_ms").std().alias("std"),
)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 4))
ax.bar(
    stats["framework"].to_list(),
    stats["avg"].to_numpy(),
    yerr=stats["std"].to_numpy(),
    capsize=4, color="#2563eb", edgecolor="black",
)
ax.set_ylabel("Latency (ms)", fontsize=12)
ax.set_title("Framework Benchmark")
fig.savefig("benchmark.pdf", bbox_inches="tight", dpi=300)

bytes.topic: Seaborn — statistical visualizations, themes, integration with Pandas
bytes.title: Polars + Seaborn — Skip the Full .to_pandas() Tax
bytes.body:
Seaborn expects Pandas DataFrames, but converting your entire Polars DataFrame with .to_pandas() is wasteful when you only need two or three columns for a plot. Filter and select first in Polars, then convert the minimal subset.

Seaborn's statistical plots — violinplot, kdeplot, pairplot — compute aggregations internally, so feeding them pre-aggregated Polars data defeats the purpose. Hand off raw (but narrowed) data instead.

For themed, publication-ready charts, call sns.set_theme() before plotting. Seaborn respects matplotlib rcParams, so Polars stays out of the styling path entirely.

One gotcha: Polars Categorical columns convert to Pandas CategoricalDtype, but the physical ordering may differ. If your Seaborn hue or x-axis order looks wrong, explicitly cast to .cast(pl.Utf8) before converting, or pass an explicit order= parameter on the Seaborn side.

For repeated plotting in notebooks, wrap the select-convert-plot cycle in a thin helper that accepts expressions, keeping your Polars-native pipeline clean while Seaborn handles the visual layer.
bytes.code_snippet:
import polars as pl
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

df = pl.read_parquet("metrics.parquet")

# narrow in Polars, convert only what Seaborn needs
subset = (
    df.filter(pl.col("status") == "complete")
    .select("latency_ms", "endpoint", "region")
    .cast({"endpoint": pl.Utf8, "region": pl.Utf8})  # avoid cat ordering issues
    .to_pandas()
)

sns.set_theme(style="whitegrid", palette="viridis")
sns.violinplot(data=subset, x="endpoint", y="latency_ms", hue="region")
plt.title("Latency Distribution by Endpoint")
plt.show()

bytes.topic: Jupyter — notebook best practices, reproducibility, parameterized notebooks
bytes.title: Polars in Jupyter — Reproducible Notebooks with Parameterized LazyFrames
bytes.body:
Jupyter notebooks rot fast when DataFrames are executed out of order. Polars' lazy API is your best defense: build the entire query plan declaratively, then call .collect() in a single cell at the end. This makes cell execution order irrelevant to correctness.

For parameterized notebooks, define all config in cell one — file paths, filter thresholds, date ranges — then reference them inside pl.scan_parquet() and .filter() expressions. Papermill can inject these parameters at runtime for batch runs.

Key practices: (1) Always use scan_* over read_* so the query plan stays lazy until you explicitly collect. (2) Pin your polars version with %pip install polars==1.30.0 in the first cell. (3) Use .explain() to inspect the optimized plan before collecting — this is your notebook's 'dry run'. (4) Avoid intermediate .collect() calls mid-pipeline; they break predicate pushdown and projection pushdown optimizations. (5) Serialize final results with .sink_parquet() for reproducible artifacts.
bytes.code_snippet:
import polars as pl

# Cell 1: Parameters (Papermill-injectable)
DATA_PATH = "s3://lake/events.parquet"
MIN_SCORE = 0.8
DATE_FROM = "2026-01-01"

# Cell 2: Lazy query plan
q = (
    pl.scan_parquet(DATA_PATH)
    .filter(pl.col("score") >= MIN_SCORE)
    .filter(pl.col("created_at") >= pl.lit(DATE_FROM).str.to_datetime())
    .group_by("category")
    .agg(pl.col("score").mean().alias("avg_score"))
)

print(q.explain())  # inspect optimized plan
result = q.collect()

bytes.topic: Performance — vectorization, chunking large files, memory-efficient operations
bytes.title: Polars Lazy + Streaming — Process 50GB Files on 8GB RAM
bytes.body:
Polars' lazy API doesn't just defer execution — it enables streaming mode, which processes data in batches without loading the full dataset into memory. This is the key to handling files larger than your available RAM.

When you call .collect(streaming=True) on a LazyFrame, Polars breaks the query into pipeline-friendly chunks, applies predicate pushdown and projection pushdown per chunk, and never materializes the full intermediate result. This means a group_by aggregation on a 50GB Parquet file can run on a laptop with 8GB RAM.

Critical detail: not all operations support streaming. Joins and sorts that require global ordering will force a full materialization. Use .explain(streaming=True) to inspect whether Polars actually streams your plan — look for STREAMING in the output.

For maximum throughput, pair streaming with scan_parquet's row_index and hive_partitioning options to skip irrelevant row groups entirely. Predicate pushdown on partitioned Parquet means Polars never reads data it doesn't need — zero wasted I/O at the storage layer.
bytes.code_snippet:
import polars as pl

# Lazy scan — nothing read yet
q = (
    pl.scan_parquet("events/**/*.parquet", hive_partitioning=True)
    .filter(pl.col("event_date") >= "2025-01-01")
    .group_by("user_id")
    .agg(
        pl.col("duration_ms").mean().alias("avg_duration"),
        pl.col("event_id").count().alias("event_count"),
    )
)

# Verify streaming is used
print(q.explain(streaming=True))

# Execute in streaming mode — constant memory
result = q.collect(streaming=True)

bytes.topic: Time Series — resampling, rolling windows, lag features, seasonality
bytes.title: Polars Time Series — Lag Features and Rolling Windows Without the Pandas Baggage
bytes.body: Polars handles time series natively through its expression API, not bolted-on methods. The group_by_dynamic method replaces pandas resample — pass every="1h" to bucket timestamps, then aggregate with any expression. For rolling windows, shift from .rolling() on a Series to .rolling_mean(window_size="7d") directly in a select or with_columns context, keyed by a sorted datetime column using by parameter. Lag features are just .shift(n) inside expressions — chain multiple shifts to build autoregressive feature sets in a single with_columns call without intermediate DataFrames. Seasonality extraction uses .dt.hour(), .dt.day_of_week(), or .dt.month() as categorical encoders. The critical Polars advantage: all of these compose lazily. A LazyFrame pipeline that resamples, adds 5 lag columns, computes a 30-day rolling std, and extracts day-of-week executes in one optimized pass. No copies, no index alignment bugs. Sort your time column first — group_by_dynamic requires it and won't warn you clearly if you forget.
bytes.code_snippet:
import polars as pl

df = pl.scan_csv("timeseries.csv").with_columns(
    pl.col("timestamp").str.to_datetime()
).sort("timestamp")

features = df.with_columns(
    pl.col("value").shift(1).alias("lag_1"),
    pl.col("value").shift(7).alias("lag_7"),
    pl.col("value").rolling_mean(window_size=7).alias("roll_7d_mean"),
    pl.col("value").rolling_std(window_size=30).alias("roll_30d_std"),
    pl.col("timestamp").dt.weekday().alias("day_of_week"),
    pl.col("timestamp").dt.hour().alias("hour"),
).drop_nulls().collect()

bytes.topic: Statistical Testing — hypothesis tests, p-values, confidence intervals, when they matter
bytes.title: Polars for Statistical Testing — Hypothesis Tests Without Leaving Your DataFrame
bytes.body:
Polars doesn't ship scipy, but its expression engine handles the heavy lifting of preparing statistical tests efficiently. Compute group means, variances, and sample sizes in a single lazy query, then pass results to scipy for the final p-value. The key advantage: Polars computes aggregations in parallel across groups before you ever touch Python-speed code.

For confidence intervals, use `col.mean()`, `col.std()`, and `col.count()` expressions to build margin-of-error calculations directly. With `group_by().agg()`, you can compute test statistics for hundreds of A/B segments in one pass — no loops, no apply.

When p-values matter: feature drift detection, A/B tests on model predictions, validating that a retrained model's error distribution hasn't shifted. When they don't: exploratory slicing where effect sizes speak for themselves.

Pro tip: use `.with_columns()` to attach confidence bounds directly to summary frames, keeping everything in a single Polars pipeline instead of juggling numpy arrays.
bytes.code_snippet:
import polars as pl
from scipy import stats

df = pl.DataFrame({"group": ["A"]*100 + ["B"]*100, "value": range(200)})

summary = df.group_by("group").agg(
    mean=pl.col("value").mean(),
    std=pl.col("value").std(),
    n=pl.col("value").count(),
).with_columns(
    ci_95=(1.96 * pl.col("std") / pl.col("n").sqrt()).alias("ci_95"),
)

rows = summary.sort("group").to_dicts()
t_stat, p_value = stats.ttest_ind_from_stats(
    rows[0]["mean"], rows[0]["std"], rows[0]["n"],
    rows[1]["mean"], rows[1]["std"], rows[1]["n"],
)
print(f"t={t_stat:.3f}, p={p_value:.4f}")

bytes.topic: Data Validation — schema validation with Pandera or Great Expectations, catching bad data early
bytes.title: Polars + Pandera — Schema Validation That Catches Bad Data Before Your Pipeline Does
bytes.body:
Pandera ships native Polars support via `pandera.polars`, letting you define column-level schemas that validate LazyFrames and DataFrames without converting to pandas. Define a `DataFrameModel` with Polars-native dtype annotations and constraints — nullability checks, value ranges, regex patterns — then call `schema.validate(lf)` directly on your LazyFrame. Validation runs against the collected result, catching dtype mismatches, null violations, and out-of-range values with clear error messages pointing to the offending column and check.

The key advantage over rolling your own `.filter()` checks: Pandera gives you declarative schemas you can version-control alongside your pipeline code. When upstream data shifts — a column goes from `Int64` to `Utf8`, or nulls appear in a previously clean field — your schema fails fast with an actionable `SchemaError` instead of silently producing garbage downstream.

For large datasets, validate a `.head(10_000)` sample in CI and the full frame in production. Pair with `lf.collect(streaming=True)` to keep memory bounded during validation of big scans.
bytes.code_snippet:
import polars as pl
import pandera.polars as pa

class EventSchema(pa.DataFrameModel):
    user_id: int = pa.Field(gt=0, nullable=False)
    event_type: str = pa.Field(isin=["click", "view", "purchase"])
    amount: float = pa.Field(ge=0.0, nullable=True)
    ts: pl.Utf8 = pa.Field(str_matches=r"^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}T")

lf = pl.scan_csv("events.csv")
try:
    validated_df = EventSchema.validate(lf)
except pa.errors.SchemaError as e:
    print(f"Validation failed: {e.failure_cases}")

bytes.topic: Working with Large Data — chunking, Dask, sampling strategies, when to graduate to Spark
bytes.title: Polars scan_parquet() — Process 100GB Files Without Blowing Up RAM
bytes.body:
Polars already handles larger-than-RAM datasets natively through its lazy API — no Dask required. The key is scan_parquet() (or scan_csv()), which creates a LazyFrame that pushes predicates and projections down to the I/O layer, reading only what's needed.

For truly massive datasets, combine lazy scanning with streaming execution via collect(streaming=True). This processes data in batches internally, keeping memory flat. Pair it with sink_parquet() to write results incrementally without materializing the full frame.

Sampling is straightforward: after a lazy scan, chain .collect().sample(n=10_000) or use .head()/slice() in the lazy plan for deterministic subsets. For stratified sampling, group_by then apply sample per group.

When to graduate to Spark? When your workload is distributed across nodes — Polars is single-node only. If your data fits on one machine (even 500GB+ with streaming), Polars will outperform Spark dramatically. The real boundary isn't data size, it's cluster necessity: joins across partitioned datasets on multiple machines.
bytes.code_snippet:
import polars as pl

# Lazy scan — reads only 'user_id' and 'event' columns,
# filters at the I/O level, never loads full file
result = (
    pl.scan_parquet("events/**/*.parquet")
    .filter(pl.col("event") == "purchase")
    .select("user_id", "event", "amount")
    .group_by("user_id")
    .agg(pl.col("amount").sum().alias("total_spend"))
    .sort("total_spend", descending=True)
    .collect(streaming=True)
)

# Stratified sample: 100 users per region
sampled = result.group_by("region").map_groups(
    lambda df: df.sample(n=min(100, len(df)))
)

bytes.topic: Reproducibility — random seeds, environment pinning, notebook-to-script conversion
bytes.title: Polars Reproducibility — Seeds, Pinning, and Escaping Notebooks
bytes.body:
Polars uses a streaming, multi-threaded engine that can reorder operations across threads. This means reproducibility requires more than just setting a Python random seed. First, pin your polars version exactly (`polars==1.30.0` in requirements.txt, not `>=`). Minor releases can change query optimizer behavior, altering row order in joins and group-bys. For deterministic row ordering, always end pipelines with an explicit `.sort()` — Polars makes zero guarantees about output order otherwise.

For random sampling, `df.sample(n=100, seed=42)` is reproducible within the same polars version, but cross-version stability isn't promised. When converting notebooks to scripts, extract your LazyFrame pipeline into a function returning a `LazyFrame`, then call `.collect()` once at the boundary. This avoids the notebook anti-pattern of repeatedly collecting intermediate DataFrames, which obscures which materialization produced your result. Lock your environment with `uv pip compile` and commit the lockfile. Reproducibility in Polars is deterministic ordering plus version pinning — the engine won't do it for you.

bytes.topic: Visualization Best Practices — choosing the right chart, color accessibility, avoiding chartjunk
bytes.title: Polars to Plot — Visualization Best Practices Without the Chartjunk
bytes.body:
Polars DataFrames don't plot themselves, but their expressive API makes pre-visualization data shaping trivial — which is where most chart mistakes originate. Before reaching for matplotlib or plotly, use `df.group_by().agg()` to pre-aggregate. Plotting raw granular data when you need summaries is the #1 source of chartjunk.

Chart selection starts with your column types. Use `df.schema` to inspect: temporal + numeric → line chart; categorical + numeric → bar chart; two numeric → scatter. Avoid pie charts for more than 4 categories — use `df.sort(col, descending=True).head(5)` to trim before plotting.

For color accessibility, extract your series count with `df.select(pl.col('category').n_unique()).item()` and choose a colorblind-safe palette sized accordingly (Okabe-Ito or IBM Design). Never rely on color alone — pair with markers or annotations.

Convert to pandas only at the plot boundary: `df.to_pandas()` keeps the heavy lifting in Polars' Rust engine. Use `cast(pl.Date)` before plotting to let matplotlib auto-format axes correctly rather than fighting string labels.
bytes.code_snippet:
import polars as pl
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

df = pl.read_csv("metrics.csv")

# Pre-aggregate in Polars, not in the plotting layer
plot_df = (
    df.group_by("category")
    .agg(pl.col("value").mean().alias("avg_value"))
    .sort("avg_value", descending=True)
    .head(6)
)

# Colorblind-safe palette sized to actual series count
palette = ["#648FFF","#785EF0","#DC267F","#FE6100","#FFB000","#000000"]
pd_df = plot_df.to_pandas()
pd_df.plot.barh(x="category", y="avg_value", color=palette[:len(pd_df)])
plt.xlabel("Avg Value")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("chart.png", dpi=150)

bytes.topic: Data Versioning — tracking dataset versions alongside model versions
bytes.title: Polars LazyFrame Hashing — Fingerprint Your Datasets for Version Tracking
bytes.body:
Every ML pipeline needs to answer: which exact dataset produced this model? Polars makes fingerprinting DataFrames trivial using its built-in hashing. By computing a deterministic hash over your LazyFrame's contents, you can tie a dataset snapshot to a model checkpoint without copying entire parquets into your artifact store.

The approach: serialize your schema and a content hash into a lightweight version manifest. Use `polars.concat_str` and `.hash_rows()` to produce a per-row hash, then reduce to a single fingerprint. Store this alongside your model metadata.

This works because Polars hashing is deterministic within the same version and architecture. Pair it with `.collect().write_parquet()` using `row_group_size` to keep snapshots reproducible. For diffing between versions, join two frames on the primary key and filter where row hashes diverge — you get a precise changeset without comparing cell-by-cell.

Critical caveat: Polars uses a non-cryptographic hash (xxhash). It's fast and collision-resistant enough for versioning, not for security.
bytes.code_snippet:
import polars as pl
from datetime import datetime

def fingerprint_dataset(lf: pl.LazyFrame, key_col: str) -> dict:
    hashed = lf.with_columns(
        pl.concat_str(pl.all().cast(pl.Utf8)).hash().alias("_row_hash")
    ).collect()
    fingerprint = hashed.select(pl.col("_row_hash").hash()).item()
    return {
        "fingerprint": str(fingerprint),
        "schema": dict(hashed.schema),
        "row_count": hashed.height,
        "created_at": datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),
    }

lf = pl.scan_parquet("training_data.parquet")
version = fingerprint_dataset(lf, key_col="sample_id")
print(version)
